mnemonics.title: PECTRA - Factors Affecting Estimation
mnemonics.content:
P - People (team experience, stability)
E - Environment (tools, technology)
C - Complexity (requirements, architecture)
T - Time (project phase, schedule pressure)
R - Resources (availability, constraints)
A - Artifacts (documentation, quality standards)

mnemonics.title: RISE - Estimation Best Practices
mnemonics.content:
R - Review historical data from past projects
I - Iteratively refine estimates as project progresses
S - Use multiple techniques (top-down + bottom-up)
E - Express uncertainty using ranges, not single numbers

acronyms.usage: An empirical estimation model that uses mathematical formulas based on size (KLOC) and cost drivers to estimate effort and schedule.
A measurement unit that counts software functionality from user's perspective, used as input for estimation models.
A mathematical model for software estimation based on Rayleigh curve theory and productivity metrics.
A estimation technique using optimistic, pessimistic, and most likely estimates to calculate expected duration.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an inverted triangle starting wide at the top (high uncertainty: estimates could be 1x to 4x actual) and narrowing downward (low uncertainty: estimates close to actual). The project timeline runs vertically, with uncertainty decreasing as you move down.
Think of a tree: Top-down starts from the trunk (total estimate) and branches down to leaves (components). Bottom-up starts from leaves (individual work packages) and builds up to the trunk (total).
A horizontal timeline showing: Project Start → Estimation provided (wide range) → Analysis done → Commitment made → Implementation → Actual result. Estimate comes BEFORE commitment.

analogies.analogy: Estimating software is like predicting the weather months in advance. You can make educated guesses based on patterns and data, but unexpected factors can dramatically change outcomes.

The Cone of Uncertainty is like planning a road trip to an unfamiliar destination: your initial estimate of time/distance could be way off, but as you get closer and learn the route, your estimates become more accurate.

Bottom-up estimation is like estimating the cost of a house by listing every material (bricks, wood, nails) and labor hour, then summing them up - tedious but more accurate than guessing from total square footage.
